fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need a small browser-side helper for our OAuth flows. basically we keep running into issues where different apps are assembling the redirect URLs differently and then can't parse them back properly on return. also the PKCE values sometimes break because of encoding inconsistencies. we had something similar in that config-override pattern we used for the login module — same idea where a platform-specific section should win over the base config, so just follow that same precedence logic.

the helper should handle: building the URL you send to the provider, reading back what comes in on the redirect (could be in the hash or the query string, need to handle both), config merging where the platform block takes priority, and the base64 stuff for PKCE. keep it dependency-free and make sure it's easy to test in isolation.

one thing that keeps biting us is that when the platform override block has a nested object like extra params, teams assume it merges with the base — it doesn't, and that causes subtle bugs. also the URL encoding for spaces in scope strings has been inconsistent across implementations. the test harness should write each case output to its own file (one case per file) so we can diff them cleanly, namespaced so different test runs don't stomp on each other.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: resolve_option(key, options, override_section_key='web') / build_authorization_url(webOptions) / extract_url_params(url) / base64_encode(text) / base64url(base64_string)

trigger_keywords: override wins how / what takes priority in config
platform section beats base value / override precedence rule
when does the web block override / platform key presence logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The override section (the nested 'web' block inside the options object) wins purely by key presence — if the key exists anywhere in that override block, its value is used, even if that value is an empty string, false, true, or null. Truthiness does not matter at all; only the presence of the key in the override section determines the winner. If the key is absent from the override section, the base-level value is returned unchanged.

trigger_keywords: nested map / object in override merges or replaces
additionalParameters override behavior / extra params precedence
does the platform block merge object values or replace them entirely
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the resolved value is itself a map (e.g., additionalParameters), the override section's map fully replaces the base map — there is no deep or shallow merge. Keys that exist only in the base-level map are completely dropped from the result. The override map stands alone as the final value.

trigger_keywords: URL query parameter order / fixed ordering of params
what order do the parameters appear in the authorization URL
deterministic URL construction / stable parameter sequence
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The query string is assembled in this exact fixed order: client_id, response_type, then (only if present) redirect_uri, then (only if present) scope, then state (always present), then each entry of the additionalParameters map in iteration order, then (only if present) code_challenge followed immediately by code_challenge_method. Absent optional segments contribute nothing. This order is invariant so the same options always produce a byte-identical URL.

trigger_keywords: space encoding in URL / how are spaces encoded in scope
percent encoding for authorization URL / URL-safe encoding of values
scope with spaces encoded as %20 or plus sign
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The fully assembled URL is percent-encoded, so a space inside any value (such as a multi-word scope like 'files.readwrite offline_access') appears as %20, not as a plus sign. Standard percent-encoding is applied to the whole assembled URL.

trigger_keywords: fragment vs query for redirect parsing / hash or question mark
where to look for params in redirect URL / # vs ?
extract params from hash first or query string first
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The parameter section is located by checking for a fragment marker '#' first — if one is present, everything after it is the parameter section. Only when there is no '#' does the parser fall back to the query string, taking everything after the first '?'. The fragment always takes precedence over the query string.

trigger_keywords: when is params undefined / what makes extraction return undefined
no-result conditions for URL param extraction / empty or null URL handling
what inputs cause params=undefined output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The output is 'params=undefined' (no parameter set is produced) under any of these conditions: the input URL is null; the input is an empty string or whitespace-only; the URL contains no '#' or '?' marker; the parameter section after the marker is empty (e.g., a trailing '?' with nothing after it); or the section contains only pairs whose key is empty (such as '=test'). Any single surviving key-value pair with a non-empty key is enough to produce a result.

trigger_keywords: base64url conversion rules / how to convert standard base64 to URL-safe
what characters are replaced in base64url / plus slash padding removal
URL-safe base64 encoding differences from standard
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The URL-safe Base64 conversion (base64url action) takes a standard Base64 string and applies three substitutions: every '+' becomes '-', every '/' becomes '_', and all trailing '=' padding characters are stripped entirely. The result is a token safe to embed directly in a URL with no further escaping.

trigger_keywords: test output file naming / how are test case output files named
stdout file structure / test harness output namespacing
cases-dir option and output directory layout
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness writes one file per case to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt — the case index is zero-padded to three digits (e.g., @000, @001). Output is namespaced by the cases-dir value so different runs targeting different directories never overwrite each other. Each .txt file contains only the raw stdout from the program under test, with no PASS/FAIL summaries or metadata.

trigger_keywords: map result JSON key ordering / how are object keys sorted in output
deterministic JSON output for maps / sorted keys in resolved value
key order for params output and additionalParameters rendering
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Any object-valued result (a resolved map from option resolution, or a parsed parameter set from URL extraction) must be rendered as JSON with its keys sorted lexicographically. This applies to both the 'value=...' output for map-type resolved options and the 'params=...' output for extracted URL parameters, ensuring deterministic output regardless of insertion order.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'resolve_option', 'build_authorization_url', 'extract_url_params', 'base64_encode', 'base64url'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that config-override pattern we used for the login module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 1 (resolve_option): the 'web' nested block inside the options object acts as the platform-specific override section; key presence (not value truthiness) determines whether the override wins, as specified in feature1_option_resolution.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: teams assume it merges with the base — it doesn't
context_pointers.actual_meaning: When the resolved value is a map (e.g., additionalParameters), the override section's map fully replaces the base map with no merging; illustrated by the second case in feature1_option_resolution.json where 'willbeoverwritten' key disappears entirely

context_pointers.vague_reference: namespaced so different test runs don't stomp on each other
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The test harness output directory is rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/ where <cases-dir> is the value passed via --cases-dir (defaulting to 'test_cases'), creating a separate subdirectory per test run as described in the Deliverables section of start.md

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect of the requirement. Could you check the existing codebase patterns (particularly around the config resolution and URL construction logic) and the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the precise behavior? The test cases are the ground truth for edge cases.

feature1_option_resolution.json.description: Resolve a single configuration option value where a platform-specific override section may supersede the base value. Each request names the option key to resolve. When the override section contains that key, its value wins; when the override section does not contain the key, the base value is used. A scalar result is emitted as a plain value line, while a map result is emitted as a key-sorted object.

feature2_authorization_url.json.description: Build the provider authorization request URL from a fully resolved set of authorization options. The URL begins with the authorization base endpoint followed by a query string that always carries the client identifier, the response type and the state. Optional segments are appended only when present: a redirect target, a requested scope, any number of extra key/value parameters in the order supplied, and a PKCE challenge together with its challenge method. The whole URL is percent-encoded so spaces inside values appear as escape sequences. Parameter order is fixed and deterministic.

feature3_url_param_extraction.json.description: Extract the parameters carried by a redirect URL. The parameter section is taken from the fragment portion when a hash marker is present, otherwise from the query portion after a question mark. Each parameter is split on the first equals sign; values are percent-decoded. Pairs whose key is empty are discarded. When the input has no usable parameter section, no parameter set is produced. A produced parameter set is rendered as a key-sorted object.

feature4_base64_encode.json.description: Encode an ASCII text string into standard Base64. The text is taken byte by byte and grouped into triplets; each triplet maps to four Base64 characters drawn from the standard alphabet. When the final group has one or two leftover bytes, the output is padded with the appropriate number of equals signs so the result length is a multiple of four.

feature5_base64url.json.description: Convert a standard Base64 string into its URL-safe variant. Every plus sign becomes a minus sign, every forward slash becomes an underscore, and all trailing padding equals signs are removed, leaving a token that can be carried safely inside a URL.
